So the basic premise of Logic is that if you have some kind of SOP or like a standard operating procedure or product requirements document, which is some kind of natural language description of a business process, you should be able to add it to our system and we'll automate that process for you.
And there's, there's implicit constraints there, but that's the high level thing.
You give us a description of a business process, we automate it for you.
When you go to create a new document, you can give us a one-line description where we'll write the document for you or where you can bring your own.
You may have also seen, if I type in marketing or something, we'll pull up some related templates that we might have in our workflow library that we have.
But in this case, let's just start with a blank document.
I have a pretty simple but small document that I tend to test things with that just describes redacting PII.
And so we can just throw this into Logic and we'll publish this.
And as it's publishing, you'll see a whole bunch of things kick off.
Okay, yeah.
This is our staging server, so there might be a couple wonky things.
And so as soon as I add just the Logic, behind the scenes, we just kicked off about 25 agents.
that are doing, kind of teasing apart this document.
The document itself is pretty simple.
It just says, you know, given a random blob of text, redact any PII from it, annotate what kind of PII was there, and give us back a list of the PII that we removed from it.
PII is personally identifiable information.
And here's a list of things that you might want to think about redacting.
And so as soon as we get to the logic, we generate a user interface for it.
This is a simple user interface.
Because this is just a single field, you know, it'd be like Erik, Azzam, and Steve are on a call.
We can now run this process right in browser.
The first time you run it, it does take an extra second or two, but this is actually being driven by this document on the left right now.
And in a second, you should see this text redacted along with a list of the things that we redacted.
And so, you know, as promised, you know, here's redacted content, here's the things we redacted from it.
If we wanted to export this as like a spreadsheet or something,
Then we could do that.
And if we just wanted to generate random data to test things with, we can do that as well.

So one of the big things, though, is we, especially for our target audience who are generally non-technical, we want to give them high confidence that this system, this process will actually work and do the things that they expect it to do and work reliably.
So we also generate test cases.
And our test case generator, I'll run these.
Our test case generator tries to get into kind of the nooks and crannies of your SOP and try to find parts that might be edge cases or maybe underspecified.
And we'll actually actively try to, like, break, for lack of a better word, your SOP and highlight areas that could be improved.
so typically, you'll see one to three, for more complex SOPs, maybe seven to all 10 might fail.
I would expect for this case, maybe one, one to three might fail.
Yeah, so in this case, one failed.
One
And usually we'll explain it to you, like the actual result redacts both the name and email address, but the expected result only redacts the email.
And so we can kind of go in and see what's going on here.
And so in the actual output, oh, so this is a scenario where the live document is actually correct and the test is wrong.
The test, like the expected output, according to the test, did not exclude Jane.
And so we could go and we really need one, like a button that would just say like, hey, fix my test or fix my doc.
And that is something on our list.
We just don't have it yet.
But today we'd go in and edit it and say like, oh, you know, the actual correct out expected output is that Jane should be redacted.

And we could we could fix up this test if it was the other way around, if the test was correct, but the document was not working correctly, we could just go over here and edit our document until the test passes.
We also generate integration docs.
We
If you have an engineering team that wants to kind of call this process as though it's an API, every single one of your documents in Logic becomes an endpoint that they can call.
Every single API is well-typed, both on the inputs and the outputs.
We guarantee anything that our system generates will match this schema.
So in this case, it's a pretty simple schema, the redacted content and the redacted entities.
We give you little code snippets.
And so I could hop over here, copy this, and if I hop over to my terminal, I can just paste this in.
And now this is actually calling the exact same back end, the exact same process that we were running in the web browser.
But now I can put this in an engineering pipeline.
And in three seconds, you'll see a wall-type JSON payload instead of kind of the web UI that we were using before.
And so, yeah, just like before, you know, here's the input, here's the output.
Behind the scenes, we do do a lot of little things.
Like when we say...
The status was successful.
That doesn't just mean that our server kind of responded to your request.
Before we ever give a response back to the user, we actually have a second agent kind of inspect the output and make sure it semantically seems correct.

And if, you know, if the inputs were weird or if the output just doesn't seem right, we'll actually flag that as an error and explain, like, hey, we couldn't do this task because you, you know, the process requires a color and you gave us a number.
And like, that's, you know, we just can't do the thing you asked us to do.
So success here means more than just like, hey, we responded to you.
Let's see.

Oh, we, you know, this is kind of a newer thing, but if you just want to, like, integrate this into an existing code base, you can actually copy this whole long agentic prompt that we have that kind of is all the context codex or cursor or cod would need to just kind of crawl your code base and do the integration on your behalf.
We've tested this on a really large code base, like 10 million plus line code bases.
And it works incredibly well.
And we also keep an execution history of everything.
So you can see all the test calls that we've done.
The artifact is the web inference that we did through the web UI.
And then this is the API call that we just made on the terminal.
And we do versioning.
And so right now, we only have one version, and it's live.
But if we were to edit this, we could go in and, I don't know, maybe add another example, maybe delete something.
And so now when I go to publish this, it won't actually go live right away.
It'll actually go to a draft, an in review state.
And we give you kind of a little red line version of what's changing.
You'll see all of the user interface, you know, the user interface will always stay up to date.
The test cases are updating, the integration docs are updating.
Anytime you make any changes to this document, we make sure everything else gets updated automatically.
So the only thing the user ever has to worry about is this document over here.
And the basic premise there is if you know how to use Google Docs or if you know how to use Notion, you can create an automated workflow in Logic.
You literally just write what you want to happen.
We take care of everything else.
Let's assume this was good.
I can approve and publish this.
And now in my versions, you'll see the previous live version.
We have a new live version.
And we automatically kicked off a new draft in this scenario.
But suppose the new live version was broken because we deleted things that we shouldn't have broken or they shouldn't have deleted.
Another thing that we give the end user is confidence that we try to give you a lot of upfront kind of artifacts to give you confidence that the process is working.
And kind of doing what you expected to do.
But if you ever do make a mistake and kind of push something.
Live that you wish hadn't gone live.
It's one click to fix it.
You just find the previous version that you knew worked.
You just click and click.
And now it's as though you'd never even published that broken version.
And so it's all about giving kind of like a non-technical audience comfort in owning these workflows that may live in your business's critical path.
So that's kind of the high-level overview of all the features we have.
Oh, well, yeah, I guess there's this too.
Like the Web UI, if you just wanted to share the user interface without the actual document, you can do that.
And so right now we do require you to log in, but we are making these shareable without having to be authenticated as well.
So if you just wanted to kind of slack a tool to your team, you could do that.
But another thing that's in flight is exposing all of these tools as, or all these loaded documents, I should say, as tools in Claude.
So you can see this is still in flight, and so this is pretty gnarly, but all of your logic documents can become a tool that Claude can call on.

And I think any other thing we're showing is maybe a more complex use case is where this use case was motivated by one of the customers that, or I guess design partners that we might be working with, a health benefits company where they get kind of messy employee data that might be like, it's not capitalized correctly, or maybe there's just minor things wrong with the data when they're ingesting new clients, and they just have a whole process that describes how to clean it up.
And so they just write over here what they want to happen.
We give their end user, or which might be their ops team or some kind of onboarding person, a tool where they can use this.
But realistically, in their case, they would probably call us through an API.
They would just call this every time.
A new client is created, and we also handle a lot of our more interesting use cases have to do with files, like PDFs or images.
And so this is a simple one where you'll receive a PDF invoice, and we want you to extract all of this from it.
We can just hop into the history and see, yeah, like a previous call.
It's like here, here's one of our invoices.
Well, this is not one of our invoices, but a sample invoice.
And then, like, you know, extracting well-structured data from it.

And this is a simple one where it's just, like, simple field extraction, but we'll have use cases where there might be validation or, like, compliance steps, like making sure certain fields match or, you know, doing something additional with this data.
So I'll pause there.
I just threw a lot at you.
I can get into, like, real customer use cases, but I'll pause there.
Oh, yeah, I'm following.
I was going to ask about uploading files, but then you went to this one.
So is there, I guess, any use cases where there's like a multi-step, like a multi-hop from like uploading the process invoice then to hand off to another automated process?
Yeah.
So right now that is like, I guess, one of the constraints on the kinds of things you can automate in the system.
Within a single document, you can have kind of logic, like branch logic, like, oh, go do this.
And if this happens, go do that.
And we do have some ability to go to, we can do like deep research in terms of like pulling in external data or doing searches on the web.
So if you ask us to research a topic, we can do that.

But in terms of kind of handing it off to another system, right now you would need some kind of wrapper, or either an integration team, or you need to use like N8N or Zapier or something to kind of shuffle data between.
One of the other things kind of on our short to midterm roadmap is the ability for like one process, one logic document to call another logic document.
And so you can kind of imagine kind of these trees or graphs of documents kind of all calling each other.
We just don't have that today.
Gotcha.
Yeah, so I guess like one more thing that would be really key to emphasize is like a really good example.
The very first customer that ever went to prod with us is this e-commerce company, Garmentory.
They're a small 14 person company.
There's one technical person on staff who also happens to be the CEO.
So the CEO writes all the code and they they've been around for a little over a decade.
And they have a copy of their moderation process for for a decade.
They had four contractors on staff that would follow this process.
Every time they ingested inventory.
Inventory from Shopify, the human contractors would follow this guide for how to clean up brand name, how to clean up product name, what do you want to do for kid stuff, what do want to do for books.
And the contractors were expected to do about 500 items each per day, so they could do about 2,000 items on any given day.
The typical time between an inventory item getting ingested and going live was about seven days.
And last Black Friday, they had a backlog of 14,000 items, and they just didn't have enough capacity to get through it.
So they gave us their SOP for how they moderate products.
It's about a 24-page SOP.
And we just gave them back an API where they could send us a product, and we respond with a moderated product.
And they just started throwing all the excess inventory at us.
And we did such a good job at it that by the middle of December, they had gotten rid of three of the four contractors, and by the middle of January, they got rid of the fourth contractor.
And so for the last...
For months, 100% of everything on Garmintory is moderated by Logic.
And since then, they have like, I don't know, eight other use cases.
Like every time an image comes in, we analyze it for, you know, is there a person in this photo?
Is this women's, men's, home style, kids?
I think they have a process where we try to find the optimal photo to show first of like all the photos that might be uploaded with an item.

But there was, so this is like one use case that has been like a huge success because in addition to allowing them to reduce their contracting team, we also made their pipeline real time and we reduced errors.
So they're, they're, they used to have about 24% of items on the website used to have some kind of moderation error, usually pretty minor.
But when you have a human doing something 500 times a day, there's just a lot of errors.
So about 24% of items used to have.
Some error, we reduced that to 2%.
And so much higher quality results, much lower latency, save them a ton of money.
Really, really good result there.
And one other use case I'll share is a company, DroneSense.
This is a little bit of a different use case.
I see an ace about this one.
Yeah, so they do software for first responders, little drone software for first responders.
And this was just their ops team.
I think I have a copy.
Yeah.
Their actual production doc looks a little different than this, but it's roughly the same.
So they have this process.
They just have an operations team.
Their COO worked with us, and one of the people that reports to the COO uses us.
And it's super simple.
Every Friday, just, or most Fridays, they drag some purchase orders into here and they want to extract data.
you guarantee that

And concretely, let's see, do I, yeah, so they get these, so our customers drone sense, one of their largest customer is Axon, and they'll get these kind of really nasty purchase orders from them, because the Axon system doesn't know how to handle recurring software.

So instead, they send out these purchase orders for 10 years at a time, and so you'll see, you know, June 3031, June 2030, June 2029, June 2028, and then in the line items for each item, they'll say, like, how many licenses they want, how many months those licenses are good for, and then within this purchase order, there's multiple products that they're buying licenses for.
And so it's a bit of a, it's a very messy purchase order, and so every Friday, one of the ops people would kind of take this, and all they want to get out of it are two tables.
They want to get this table.
And this table, and they would spend hours doing this every Friday, and we said, hey, just describe what you're doing in a document, and we'll take it away for you.
And so that's what we did, and it's a simple interface.
They drop a PDF in here, and we give them back their tables.
But since May, they only use this a dozen or so times a month.
They use this every single month.
From a product pricing perspective, do you guys do it by document or by call each month?
Yeah, so that's something we're still iterating on.
The only customer we're actively invoicing today is Garmentory, just because they're calling us about 190,000 times every month.
And so there's this real meaningful expense there.
And for them, we started billing in June, and for the first four months, we just agreed to flat rate billing.
And then we're billing per execution.
Let me see if I have, I'll say that the, our pricing strategy is something we're still working on.
I don't, yeah, oh yeah, there it is, wait.
But our current thinking around pricing is per task.
If it's just kind of data, like data in, data out, we can do it pretty fast, pretty cheap.
Once we get into like handling images and PDFs, there's a lot more we're doing behind the scenes.
So pricing is a little bit differently and also depending on the complexity of the task.
And then, and we're just getting into tool use where we can do like deep research, like I mentioned earlier, and some other tools around like image editing and things.
But the pricing there is so variable that we just have the price per tool call.
And then the platform fee is really, we've gone back and forth on like, whether or
This should be mandatory.

We're working on a self-serve flow where I think this doesn't make sense, but having an upgrade path to, hey, if you want log retention or if you want a Slack channel with us, some of them are like enterprise, like SSO, then a platform fee comes into play.
But I'll say we've kind of done three iterations on pricing, and I'm sure we'll do eight more before we land on how to price this.
Yeah.
I'm thinking about kind of the, some of the users you guys have had, is there, I know it's like non-technical team empowerment.
Is there any hesitation from those types of users to kind of jump in and create these, given that like they spit out an API?
Yeah, that's, yeah, that's a good, quick insight.
So the, one of the reasons we started generating web user interfaces is up Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Until probably May or so, we only generated an API.
And so we had assumed that you'd always have some kind of internal engineering partner that could do some kind of integration.
And at the time, kind of our prototypical example was I was kind of pulling on examples from previous employers.

Like at Brax, we had a fraud ops team where like a credit card transaction, like a customer might swipe their card, a transaction comes in, and there's like a fixed set of things that we would analyze that transaction for that our fraud ops team would really own the source of truth for that.
But whenever, if there was like a new threat developing, fraud would have to write up what that threat looks like and then hand it over to engineering.
And then engineering would codify that into a set of rules and we'd deploy it.
And the time between like identifying a new threat to getting a rule in production that could kind of neuter that or neutralize.
Fathom was on the order of like seven to 10 days.
And the whole premise with Logic was like, well, it only takes fraud ops, like an hour to do the write-up.
Why can't they just click save and then just have it go live?
And so a lot of our early assumptions were like, you'd have an ops team and you'd have an engineering team.
And typically, the source code would be the source of truth of like what's actually happening in fraud.
And we were like, what if we could invert that?
What if the document that ops works on is the source of truth?
And then engineering just calls that document as though it's an API.
And so we kept pulling on that thread.
it's like, well, engineering has never been in that world where they've had to hand over control to a different team before.
So what else do we need to build to get them comfortable with that?
And so that's why we started doing automated testing.
That's why we have all type schemas.
That's why we have versioning and rollbacks and all of that.
Because it was like, what would engineering need to be comfortable living in that world, handing over the reins to somebody else?
That's That's
And that resonates with a good chunk of the folks that we talked to.
But then we started getting customers like DroneSense where there are no engineering counterparts.
It's just an ops team that has this process that's a pain in the , and they just want to tell an LLM to do it and do it reliably a thousand times.
And so we started thinking, like, how can we address that kind of cohort of users?
And we started generating user interfaces for the processes.

And that's why we're also working on things like MCP, like getting the tools in Cloud, so that if you're just having a conversation with your agent, and the agent is like, hey, there's actually this logic document that your company has that can help with this.
The agent will just automatically call it.
We're also, we've done some early prototyping.
It's nowhere near ready for GA, but working on a Slack bot as well.

So if you're just having a conversation and, a channel in Slack, and there happens to be a logic process or a logic document that can help you with it, the logic bot will just chime in and say, like, hey, you need me, you need me to, like.
Research this customer, or you need me to redact that information, or parse this invoice, like, hey, I got you, I'm on it.
Nice.
I had another question, Amari.
Oh, so you guys mentioned your focus is on fintech, e-commerce, and is it logistics?
Yeah.
the three primary that you guys have, I don't know if it's quite intentional yet, or if it's just more of you guys have kind of fallen into those spaces.

Yeah, it's almost entirely just from my network, because from Brax, I have a lot of fintech connections, and from Convoy, I have a lot of logistics connections, and Garmentory, I was already friendly with the CEO, but through him, I've just, like, got a couple of e-commerce connections.
So, just a coincidence of network.
Yeah.
Okay.
I was wondering, I mean, prior to joining Grotex, I did AI strategy for, like, mid-market companies, and then.
Just like private equity seems like a hot space because they don't have technical teams for the most part, but they do have a lot of processes.
I was curious if that falls into kind of your bucket of fintech or if you guys are kind of looking at professional services.
Yeah, so, well, professional services as in like.
County, legal, consulting.
Yeah.
I'd say we wouldn't say no to somebody wanting to use us for those use cases.
we had a consultant that, a solo consultant that works with law firms and, you know, she asked us like, hey, can your system process like thousand page bankruptcy PDFs and things like this?
And we're open to, we don't have to pigeonhole ourselves to just the three verticals, e-commerce, logistics, and fintech.
That just happens to be where we've gotten some early traction.
Yeah, I would think of like a good way to work.
articulate what's in common between these things is like they're operationally intensive technology forward types of organizations, right?

So you could think about like some, you know, B2C consumer SaaS company wanting to use something like this for something, but it's unlikely that it would be as urgently required as if someone was reviewing product inventory or analyzing invoices or, you know, analyzing and moderating chat in a food delivery app.
or whatever the thing is, we're just like a large number of decisions being made constantly that are actually mission critical for the product to succeed and actually execute.
So there's probably more than more of those types of products and industries than the two or three that we've identified here.
And those are just, yeah, like Steve said, lowest hanging fruit that comes to mind and that we have like the network for it.
But there's no need for us to limit ourselves to those things.
Gotcha.
Gotcha.
And then I guess within your...
Current kind of users and buyers, is there a typical champion or is it kind of just whoever has the biggest main point that you guys can solve?
Yeah, I mean, I think this is skewed because it's coming from my network.
I'm typically coming in through the C-suite.
I suspect that's not going to be the long-term champion in a company.
There have been a couple, like Azzam, range.com where an engineering manager reached out where they're building.
They just have a ton of different, like range.com is an automated wealth advisement platform and they just have a ton of documents and things that need to parse.
And an EM that I used to work with reached out and like, hey, can Logic help us with this?
And that's, but that's one of the rare scenarios where we kind of came in the middle of the org versus top-down.
Gotcha.
I would imagine long-term that it's like business users that have pain points.
It's...
It's marketing people and sales people and HR people that are just annoyed by doing a thing manually a ton of times and want a way to do it more scalably.
And part of the way that I imagine we're going to want to build out the whole go-to-market motion is very PLG-oriented.
If you have a problem, go in and start solving it today, and then we'll figure out contract and pricing and stuff once you're happy and having good experience there.
I think in the kickoff, guys mentioned Time Warner and another large enterprise that you guys are like, probably not now.
Yeah.
Can you just talk me through kind of what went into that decision?
Yeah, the other large enterprise was GM.
And also, earlier I mentioned Dronesense, how Axon is one of their clients.
We also spent a couple months talking with Axon directly, which is like the body camera company and like Taser company.
Part of it is just like the procurement process.
This is very heavy-handed and very slow.

Part of it is, like, with GM, they wanted a very cool use case where they have machines on factory floors that have these sensors, and when they break, technicians walk up and, like, pull the sensor data and interpret that sensor data in the context of some technical manual.
And they were like, hey, can we just put that technical manual in Logic, send you the sensor data, and you give us, like, a preliminary diagnosis?
Like, absolutely, 100%.
That's, like, right in a wheelhouse.
And then they asked if we could do that without access to the internet, because the factories are air-gapped, like, have no internet connection.
And I was like, oh, we can't do that.
And we could probably, like, cobble something together for them, but it's, like, it's just a distraction.
And so, like, so, you know, that didn't make sense for us.
And then Time Warner was an interesting one.
We came in, and as we kind of got into it, this was actually related.
We came in through the data science team, oddly, and which sometimes...
Sometimes people want us to do data analytics for them, and I don't think we're probably not the best solution for that.

We're more about, I think, as I mentioned earlier, automating decisions, and typically decisions that if a human is looking at some data and making this yes-no call in two minutes or less, we can probably completely automate that.
But 99% of the time, a machine should be doing that.
In the Time Warner scenario, it just had to do with more data analysis use cases.
then there was already a team internally.
There were three teams internally working on similar things, and it just felt like we were competing with some weird turf ownership boundary, big company nonsense.
And then in parallel, the champion for us in the company was kind of expressing gratitude that we're engaging at all.
But he's like, just so you know, if we do move forward, it's going to be an 18-month procurement process.
And so that was just another scenario where I was like...
Uh-huh.-huh.-huh.
You know, too much of a distraction.
Like, we'll talk again later.
Yeah.
Okay.
Yeah, was just curious if it was more like the use cases weren't a fit or, but it sounds like it's just more of the enterprise procurement and requirements.
Yeah.

And so, like, if there was, like, a rogue manager or IC at Time Warner that just, like, created a Logic account and wanted to start using us and then, you know, put us on the company card at some point, I would not be against that at all.
And, I would love for that scenario to happen over and over again, but I don't, I just think, like, going in through official channels and, like, would be a distraction for us right now.
Yeah.
Awesome.
I think that's most of the kind of product and customer questions I've got.
But I guess on the more, like, looking forward into content.
You guys have a bunch of workflows that you were showing there that pop up as soon as you start typing.
Is that something you guys want to externalize or have you externalized any of that?
Yeah, so I mean those serve a couple different purposes.

One is kind of helping users get past the blank canvas problem because we've had users come in and the first thing they do, they'll type like, oh, I want you to take all my contacts in Salesforce and then send emails to everybody and do X, Y, and Z.
And it's tough to know.
I think I mentioned this on the other call.
If your use case fits within our constraints, it's a magical experience.
But as soon as you fall out outside of those constraints, it can be frustrating because you're asking us to do something.
And we don't do a good job of relaying to you that we can't do that thing.
And so the workflow library was one to kind of give people motivation.
on some of the things you can do with Logic.
Also kind of give them a sense of the kinds of tasks that we're good at.

Also, when we're generating user interfaces and schemas and things like that, behind the scenes, we actually will find similar workflows to the one that you're working on from the workflow library and use those as examples to the LLM so the LLM can see like what other great interfaces and what other great schemas use.
So there's like there's like behind the scenes implications of it.
But to your specific question, like, yes, we do want to externalize those a bit.
